name: Potassium bromate
concern: Possibly carcinogenic to humans (IARC Group 2B). Linked to kidney and thyroid tumours in animal studies.

regulatory.FSSAI: Banned as a food additive in India (removed from the list of permitted additives in 2016).
regulatory.FDA_CFSAN: Permitted in the USA with limits; listed under California Proposition 65.
regulatory.EFSA: Not permitted for use in the European Union.

advice: Choose breads and bakery items labelled 'bromate-free'.

name: Potassium iodate
concern: Often used alongside bromate as a dough conditioner; excess intake raises iodine-overload concerns.

regulatory.FSSAI: Banned as a food additive in India (2016), alongside potassium bromate.
regulatory.EFSA: Not permitted in the European Union.

advice: Prefer bakery items without added dough conditioners.

name: Sodium nitrite
concern: In processed meats it can form nitrosamines, which are carcinogenic. Processed meat is IARC Group 1.

regulatory.IARC: Processed meat (preserved with nitrites) classified Group 1 - carcinogenic to humans.
regulatory.FSSAI: Permitted only in specified cured/processed meat products within strict limits.
regulatory.FDA_CFSAN: Permitted with limits in cured meats.

advice: Limit processed/cured meats; prefer fresh meat.

name: Sodium nitrate
concern: Can convert to nitrite and form nitrosamines in cured meats (IARC Group 1 processed meat).

regulatory.FSSAI: Permitted only in specified products within limits.

name: Butylated hydroxyanisole
concern: Possibly carcinogenic to humans (IARC Group 2B).

regulatory.FSSAI: Permitted as an antioxidant within prescribed limits.
regulatory.FDA_CFSAN: Generally Recognised As Safe (GRAS) within limits; listed under California Prop 65.

advice: Prefer snacks using natural antioxidants such as tocopherols (vitamin E).

name: Butylated hydroxytoluene
concern: Animal studies show tumour-promoting effects at high doses; classified IARC Group 3 (insufficient evidence).

name: Tertiary butylhydroquinone
concern: High intake linked to adverse effects in animal studies; commonly used to extend shelf-life of fried snacks.

regulatory.FDA_CFSAN: Permitted with an upper limit (0.02% of oil/fat content).

name: Tartrazine
concern: Linked to hyperactivity in children and allergic reactions in sensitive individuals.

regulatory.EFSA: Requires the warning 'may have an adverse effect on activity and attention in children' (Southampton colours).

advice: Prefer products coloured with natural sources (turmeric, beetroot, annatto).

name: Sunset Yellow FCF
concern: Southampton colour linked to hyperactivity in children; possible allergic reactions.

regulatory.EFSA: Southampton colour - warning label required in the EU.

name: Carmoisine
concern: Southampton colour associated with hyperactivity in children.

regulatory.EFSA: Southampton colour - warning label required in the EU.

name: Ponceau 4R
concern: Southampton colour linked to hyperactivity in children; banned in some countries.

regulatory.EFSA: Southampton colour - warning label required in the EU.
regulatory.FDA_CFSAN: Not permitted for food use in the USA.

name: Allura Red AC
concern: Southampton colour linked to hyperactivity in children; emerging research on gut inflammation.

regulatory.EFSA: Southampton colour - warning label required in the EU.

name: Monosodium glutamate
concern: Generally considered safe by regulators, but can trigger headaches/flushing in sensitive individuals and encourages overconsumption of salty snacks.

regulatory.FSSAI: Permitted; not allowed in foods for infants below 12 months; label must declare 'Contains added MSG'.

advice: Limit if sensitive; prefer snacks without added flavour enhancers.
sources: FSSAI Food Safety and Standards Regulations

name: Aspartame
concern: Classified by IARC as Group 2B (possibly carcinogenic to humans) in 2023; not for people with phenylketonuria (PKU).

regulatory.JECFA: Acceptable Daily Intake retained at 40 mg/kg body weight.
regulatory.FSSAI: Permitted within prescribed limits; PKU warning required.

advice: Moderate intake; people with PKU must avoid.

name: Acesulfame potassium
concern: Often combined with aspartame; long-term high-dose effects debated.
advice: Moderate intake of artificially sweetened products.

name: Saccharin
concern: Historically linked to bladder tumours in rats; later delisted as a human carcinogen (IARC Group 3).

regulatory.IARC: Group 3 - not classifiable as to carcinogenicity to humans.

name: Sodium benzoate
concern: Can react with ascorbic acid (vitamin C) to form benzene, a known carcinogen, especially in acidic drinks.

regulatory.FDA_CFSAN: Permitted; benzene formation monitored in soft drinks.

advice: Be cautious with acidic drinks that also list vitamin C.
sources: FDA data on benzene in beverages

name: Potassium sorbate
concern: Generally regarded as one of the safer preservatives; rare skin/allergic reactions.

name: Titanium dioxide
concern: EFSA concluded in 2021 it can no longer be considered safe as a food additive due to possible genotoxicity; banned in the EU from 2022.

regulatory.EFSA: No longer considered safe as a food additive (2021).
regulatory.EU: Banned as a food additive from 2022.
regulatory.FSSAI: Permitted within prescribed limits in certain foods.

advice: Prefer confectionery without added whitening colour.

name: Caramel colour Class IV
concern: May contain 4-methylimidazole (4-MEI), classified IARC Group 2B (possibly carcinogenic).
advice: Limit dark colas and heavily coloured sauces.

name: Carrageenan
concern: Degraded carrageenan (poligeenan) is a concern, but food-grade carrageenan is permitted; some studies suggest gut inflammation.

name: Sulphur dioxide
concern: Can trigger severe reactions in asthmatics and sulphite-sensitive individuals.

regulatory.FSSAI: Permitted within prescribed limits; declaration required.

name: Sodium metabisulphite
concern: Releases sulphur dioxide; can trigger asthma and allergic reactions.

name: Partially hydrogenated vegetable oil
concern: Industrial trans fats strongly raise cardiovascular disease risk; the WHO recommends elimination from the food supply.

regulatory.WHO: Recommends elimination of industrial trans fats.
regulatory.FSSAI: Industrial trans fat in oils and fats capped at 2% (since 2022).

name: Palm oil
concern: High in saturated fat; refining at high temperatures can form process contaminants (3-MCPD, glycidyl esters).

regulatory.EFSA: Flagged glycidyl esters (process contaminants) as a concern.

advice: Prefer products using unrefined or healthier oils where possible.
sources: EFSA opinion on process contaminants in palm oil (2016)

name: High fructose corn syrup
concern: Excess intake associated with obesity, fatty liver and metabolic syndrome.

regulatory.FSSAI: Permitted; added sugars discouraged by dietary guidelines.

name: Acrylamide
concern: Forms in starchy foods cooked at high temperatures; classified IARC Group 2A (probably carcinogenic to humans).

regulatory.EFSA: Confirmed as a public-health concern; benchmark levels set.

advice: Limit deep-fried and over-browned starchy snacks.

name: Maida
concern: Refined flour is stripped of fibre and nutrients; high-glycaemic and linked to metabolic issues when consumed in excess.
